text:
The drift diffusion model is an essential tool for inferring the latent psychological processes that govern decision-making and linking them to neural mechanisms through response time data. While several open-source software packages exist to fit these models, traditional estimation methods often require large amounts of data to yield reliable results and typically only provide point estimates of parameters. Hierarchical Bayesian estimation offers a powerful alternative; by simultaneously estimating individual subject parameters and the group-level distributions from which they are drawn, these methods increase statistical power and provide a comprehensive measure of uncertainty via the posterior distribution.

In this paper, we introduce HDDM (Hierarchical Drift Diffusion Model), a novel Python-based toolbox designed for the fast and flexible estimation of the drift diffusion model and the related linear ballistic accumulator model. HDDM is designed to be robust against outliers and requires significantly fewer observations per subject or condition than non-hierarchical approaches. Furthermore, the toolbox supports complex analyses, such as assessing how trial-by-trial neural measurements (e.g., fMRI) influence decision parameters. After outlining the theoretical foundations of the drift diffusion model and Bayesian inference, we demonstrate the utility of the toolbox using a real-world dataset. Finally, parameter recovery simulations show that HDDM outperforms traditional fitting techniques, such as maximum likelihood estimation and the $\chi^2$-quantile method. The software and its documentation are available for download at: http://ski.clps.brown.edu/hddm_docs/
